---
title: "Basic Mathematical Concepts"
subtitle: "Learn R with Dr. Hu and His Friends"
author: "Yue Hu (Department of Political Science, Tsinghua University)"
---

{{< include _extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

# Basic Mathematical Concepts {#sec-algebra-en}

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter Highlights"}

1. Basic algebra
2. Vectors
3. Matrices and equations

*Research question*: What is the underlying logic of data analysis in R?

:::

## Basic Operations

- R's arithmetic symbols match math: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`
- Type expressions directly in the Console to compute

```{webr}
#| label: algebraBasics

# The four basic operations
1 + 2
3 - 4
5 * 6
7 / 8

# Compound expression
(2 + 4) / (5 - 3)
```

---

::: {.callout-tip title="A note on whitespace"}

- Whitespace does not affect computation, but improves readability
- The Tidyverse style guide recommends consistent spacing and line breaks
- Packages `styler` and `air` reformat code in a single click

:::

```{webr}
#| label: woSpace

# Irregular spacing — R still runs it correctly
(2+4) / (  5-  3            )
```

---

- Powers `^`, modulo `%%`, logarithms `log()` / `log10()`, exponentials `exp()`
- Common abbreviations: `sqrt()` (square root), `abs()` (absolute value)

```{webr}
#| label: moreAlgebra

10^2
5 %% 3
log(11)
log10(100)
exp(12)

# Two ways to take a square root
9^(1/2)
sqrt(9)

# Absolute value
abs(-9)
```

---

- Trigonometric functions: `sin()`, `cos()`, `tan()`, `acos()`, `asin()`, `atan()`
- `pi` is R's pre-stored constant for π
- Other pre-stored constants: `letters`, `LETTERS`, `month.name`, `month.abb`

```{webr}
#| label: trigFunctions

sin(pi)
cos(pi)
tan(pi / 2)
acos(1)
asin(1)
atan(0.5)
```

---

::: {.callout-important title="Scientific notation"}

- `e` connects the coefficient and the power of 10
- Example: 200 = 2e2; 0.002 = 2e-3

:::

---

- Boolean algebra: `TRUE` / `FALSE` (or `T` / `F` for short)
- Logical connectives: `&` (and), `|` (or), `!` (not)
- The Boolean empty set: `NULL`
- Comparison operators: `==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`

```{webr}
#| label: boolean

# Equalities
1 == 2
1 == TRUE   # TRUE / FALSE behave as 1 / 0 with logical attributes
0 != F

# Inequalities
1 != 2
1 > 2
1 < 2
1 >= 2
1 <= 2

# Boolean operations
3 > 2 & 2 > 1
12 & 2 > 2
-3 | 2 > 2
```

---

- Order of Boolean evaluation: comparisons first, then logical connectives
- A bare value in a Boolean expression is treated as a self-equality check

::: {.callout-tip title="Why the double equal sign?"}

- `==` is for equality testing; `=` is reserved for assignment
- Beginners often confuse them — practice fixes it quickly

:::

## Vectors

- Vector: a sequence of values with direction
- Three ways to create: `c()`, `:` (consecutive integers), `seq()`

```{webr}
#| label: sequence

# Method 1: c() — most general
a <- c(11, 12, 13, 14, 15)
# Method 2: colon — only for consecutive integers
a1 <- 11:15
# Method 3: seq() — most flexible
a2 <- seq(from = 11, to = 15, by = 1)
a3 <- seq(11, 15)

# Verify all three are identical
a == a1
a == a2
a == a3
```

---

- Vector indexing: `[]` to extract a subset
- `a[2]` picks the 2nd element; `a[c(3, 4)]` picks multiple

```{webr}
#| label: vector

# Create a demo vector
a <- c(11, 12, 13, 14, 15)

# Single element (by position)
a[2]
a[5]

# Multiple elements (position vector inside [])
a[c(3, 4)]
```

---

::: {.callout-important title="The assignment arrow `<-`"}

- `<-` is R's standard assignment operator; the arrow points to the storage name
- Keyboard shortcut: `Alt + -` on Windows / Linux, `Option + -` on macOS
- R is essentially the only popular language using `<-` — a legacy of APL's keyboard layout

:::

---

- Vector addition / subtraction: element-wise on corresponding positions
- Scalar with vector: scalar applies to each element

```{webr}
#| label: vectorAdd

a <- c(11, 12, 13, 14, 15)

# Vector with scalar
b <- a + 4
c <- a - 0.5

# Vector with vector
a + b
a - c
```

---

- Element-wise (Hadamard) product: `*`, multiply corresponding positions
- Inner (dot) product: `%*%`, returns a scalar
- Outer (tensor) product: `%o%` or `outer()`, returns a matrix

```{webr}
#| label: vecTimes

# Element-wise product: (a1*b1, a2*b2, ...)
a * b

# Inner product: sum(a*b), scalar
a %*% b

# Outer product: A[i,j] = a[i] * b[j]
a %o% b
```

---

::: {.callout-tip title="Vector \"non-standard\" operations"}

- In R, `a / b` is element-wise division (mathematically vectors have no division)
- Recycling rule: shorter vector is reused in cycles to match the longer one

:::

```{webr}
#| label: vectorDiv

# Element-wise division: (a1/b1, a2/b2, ...)
a / b

# Verify reversibility: d = 313 * a, then d / a should all be 313
d <- a * 313
d / a
```

---

```{webr}
#| label: recycle

# A short vector (length 2)
e <- c(1, 2)

# Recycled to (1, 2, 1, 2, 1) to match a
a * e
```

- `c()` also builds character vectors — wrap each element in double quotes

```{webr}
#| label: character

# Character vector
aSentence <- c("I", "am", "from", "People's Republic of China", ".")

aSentence
```

## Section Exercises {.unnumbered}

**Exercise 1**: The recycling rule

Given the assignments of `a` and `e`, explain and compute the result of each expression.

::: { .hint exercise="recycle_rule" }

- `a` has length 5, `e` has length 2
- R recycles `e` to `c(1, 2, 1, 2, 1)`
- Then performs the operation element-wise

:::

```{webr}
#| label: recycle_rule
#| exercise: recycle_rule

a <- 11:15
e <- c(1, 2)

a - e
a / e
```

::: { .solution exercise="recycle_rule" }

```r
a <- 11:15
e <- c(1, 2)

# e is recycled to (1, 2, 1, 2, 1) to match a's length
# a - e: 11-1, 12-2, 13-1, 14-2, 15-1 = 10 10 12 12 14
a - e

# a / e: 11/1, 12/2, 13/1, 14/2, 15/1 = 11 6 13 7 15
a / e
```

:::

---

**Exercise 2**: Derive the Sexagenary Cycle

China's traditional calendar pairs ten Heavenly Stems with twelve Earthly Branches into a 60-pair cycle. Rule: Yang stems (odd positions) pair only with Yang branches; Yin stems (even positions) only with Yin branches.

::: { .hint exercise="ganzhi" }

- Use `seq()` with `%%` to separate odd / even positions (Yang / Yin)
- Use `outer()` to generate all pairwise combinations
- Use `paste0()` to concatenate two strings into one

:::

```{webr}
#| label: ganzhi
#| exercise: ganzhi

tiangan <- c("甲", "乙", "丙", "丁", "戊", "己", "庚", "辛", "壬", "癸")
dizhi <- c("子", "丑", "寅", "卯", "辰", "巳", "午", "未", "申", "酉", "戌", "亥")


```

::: { .solution exercise="ganzhi" }

```r
tiangan <- c("甲", "乙", "丙", "丁", "戊", "己", "庚", "辛", "壬", "癸")
dizhi <- c("子", "丑", "寅", "卯", "辰", "巳", "午", "未", "申", "酉", "戌", "亥")

# Extract Yang stems (odd positions) and Yin stems (even positions)
tg_yang <- tiangan[seq(tiangan) %% 2 == 1]
tg_yin <- tiangan[seq(tiangan) %% 2 == 0]

# Extract Yang branches (odd positions) and Yin branches (even positions)
dz_yang <- dizhi[seq(dizhi) %% 2 == 1]
dz_yin <- dizhi[seq(dizhi) %% 2 == 0]

# Yang stems pair with Yang branches
outer(tg_yang, dz_yang, FUN = paste0)
# Yin stems pair with Yin branches
outer(tg_yin, dz_yin, FUN = paste0)
```

:::

## Matrices

- Matrix: two-dimensional, composed of multiple vectors
- Created with `matrix()`, filled column by column (column-major order)
- Specify `nrow` or `ncol` (only one — R computes the other)

```{webr}
#| label: matrix

# A 2-row matrix; data fill column by column
matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), nrow = 2)
```

---

- Matrices also follow the recycling rule

```{webr}
#| label: matrixRecycle

# A 2-row matrix from a length-3 vector
matrix(1:3, nrow = 2)
```

---

- Matrix indexing: `A[row, col]`
- A blank position means "all"

```{webr}
#| label: matrixCell

# Build matrix A first
A <- matrix(1:3, nrow = 2)

# Single cell: row 1, column 2
A[1, 2]

# Entire row 1
A[1, ]
# Entire column 2
A[, 2]
```

---

- Transposition: `t()`, swaps rows and columns
- Two transpositions return the original

```{webr}
#| label: matrixTrans

# Original matrix
A
# One transposition
t(A)
# Two transpositions (back to original)
t(t(A))
```

---

::: {.callout-important title="Nested function calls"}

- "Inside-out" evaluation: innermost function runs first, then feeds the outer
- Analogous to mathematical function composition $g(f(x))$

:::

---

- Matrix addition / subtraction: requires matching dimensions
- Matrix multiplication: element-wise `*`, inner `%*%` (cols of first = rows of second), outer `%o%`, cross-product `crossprod()`

```{webr}
#| label: matrixTimes

# Create matrices of different dimensions
B <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2)
C <- matrix(6:1, nrow = 2)
D <- matrix(6:1, nrow = 3)

# Inspect them
B
C
D

# Element-wise operations require matching dimensions
B + C
B - C

# Multiplication varieties
B * C            # element-wise (Hadamard)
B %*% D          # inner product
B %o% C          # outer product
crossprod(B, C)  # cross-product: t(B) %*% C
```

---

- `solve()` solves a linear system
- The matrix form of a linear system parallels the matrix form of a regression

```{webr}
#| label: solve

# Coefficient matrix B (3x3)
# Coefficients of a, b, c in the three equations
B <- matrix(c(1, 3, 2, 1, 2, 2, 1, 4, -6), nrow = 3)
B

# Right-hand side vector y
y <- c(6, 9, 4)

# Solve B %*% x = y for x (i.e., the values of a, b, c)
x <- solve(B, y)
x
```

## Chapter Recap

::: {.callout-note title="A friendly reminder"}

This chapter and the chapters that follow will cover quite a few concepts.
Some of them you do not need to master at first sight — a general impression is enough, and many will deepen as you encounter them again in later chapters.
Others, however, form the core foundation of this stage. Missing them now will hinder your understanding later.
Each chapter therefore ends with a key-point recap that flags what you must hold onto at this moment.

:::

---

- Assignment: `<-`
- Boolean logic
    - Comparison: `==` (equal), `!=` (not equal), `>`, `<`
    - Connectives: `&` (and), `|` (or), `!` (not)
- Vectors and indexing
    - Build a vector: `c()`
    - Generate a sequence: `seq()`
    - Index: `[]`
- Matrices
    - Create: `matrix()`
    - Inspect dimensions: `nrow()`, `ncol()`
    - Transpose: `t()`
    - Solve / invert: `solve()`
